title: Death Verification & Certification
oneLiner: Confirm irreversible loss of brainstem function, follow legal documentation, and avoid declaring death in reversible causes (hypothermia, overdose, hypothyroidism).

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Reversible mimic — severe hypothermia (core temp <30 °C) or drug overdose (opioids, benzos, tricyclics):**  
  The single finding that says "this is a killer, not death": **pupils may be fixed/dilated, but deep tendon reflexes or spontaneous respiratory effort (even gasping) can still be present. Do NOT pronounce death until core temp >32 °C and drug effect is ruled out.**
- **Brainstem death mimic — locked-in syndrome or severe Guillain‑Barré:**  
  The single finding: **patient appears unresponsive but can make vertical eye movements. Elicit sustained upward gaze before pronouncing.**
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal signs:**  
  - No palpable pulse (carotid/femoral) for ≥5 minutes  
  - No audible heart sounds  
  - Fixed, dilated pupils unresponsive to light  
  - No respiratory effort for ≥5 minutes  
  - No response to verbal or painful stimuli  
  - Absent corneal, gag, and cough reflexes  
  - No spontaneous movement  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Apnoea test (if performed) – no respiratory effort after stopping ventilation for 5 minutes (only in controlled setting)  
  - Pupils mid‑position (4–6 mm) or fully dilated, no light reflex  
  - Absent oculocephalic ("doll's eye") and oculovestibular (caloric) reflexes  
  - No motor response to supraorbital or nail‑bed pressure
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Death vs. reversible coma (most likely mimic):**  
  - Drug overdose (e.g., opioids, sedatives) – pinpoint pupils (opioids) or flaccid tone, history of access to medications  
  - Hypothermia – core temp <30 °C, shivering may be absent  
  - Severe hypoglycaemia – serum glucose <3 mmol/L, rapid correction restores signs  
  - Hypothyroidism (myxoedema coma) – bradycardia, hypothermia, hypoventilation, thyroid history  
  - Brainstem stroke – may spare some reflexes (e.g., preserved blinking to threat)  
- **Splitter:**  
  **The single clinical feature that differentiates death from reversible coma:** **respiratory drive.** In death – no spontaneous breaths for ≥5 minutes. In reversible coma – gasping or irregular breaths (e.g., Biot’s, Cheyne‑Stokes) may be present. Also: **pupils reactive to light (any constriction) essentially rules out death.**
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - Check core temperature (rectal, bladder, oesophageal) – must be >32 °C before verification  
  - Continuous ECG or cardiac monitor – look for any electrical activity (P waves, QRS complexes)  
  - Capillary blood glucose – <3 mmol/L treat first (cannot verify death until glucose >3.5 mmol/L)  
  - Pupillary light test with bright torch  
  - Corneal reflex  
  - Gag/cough reflex (suction catheter)  
  - Pain response (supraorbital or nail‑bed pressure)  
  - 5‑minute apnoea observation (spontaneous breathing trial if ventilated – but in non‑ICU setting, just observe)  
- **Bloods:** (only if reversible cause suspected)  
  - Thyroid function, cortisol, toxicology screen  
- **Imaging:**  
  - None for verification (but consider CT head if brainstem death suspected in ICU)  
- **Special tests:**  
  - No routine ancillary tests; in ICU context, may use EEG or angiography for brainstem death  
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
  Use **UK Academy of Medical Royal Colleges (AoMRC) criteria** for confirmation of death (2019 update):  
  - Absence of conscious response  
  - Absent pupillary, corneal, gag, cough, and oculocephalic reflexes  
  - Absent motor responses in cranial nerve distribution  
  - Apnoea (no respiratory effort after disconnecting ventilator for >5 minutes with adequate oxygenation)  
- **Never‑miss test:**  
  **Core temperature measurement** – hypothermia can mimic death and cause fixed pupils/bradycardia. A cold patient is not dead until warm.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **Do NOT pronounce death until reversible causes excluded.**  
  - Check for signs of life for at least 5 minutes (pulse, breathing, pupils, reflexes).  
  - If any sign of life present → start CPR, treat reversible cause (e.g., naloxone for opioid, 50 mL 50% dextrose for hypoglycaemia).  
  - If no signs of life → call supervising clinician or consultant to confirm (if unsure).  
  - If legally dead: record exact time of death, prepare death certificate.  
- **Ward (first 24‑72h):**  
  - No active management for the deceased. For the family: offer private time, bereavement support, ensure proper viewing.  
  - Arrange for “last offices” (patient dignity, removal of lines/tubes if no coroner involvement).  
  - **Certify and complete Medical Certificate of Cause of Death (MCCD) within 24 hours if cause is natural and clear.**  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Cremation paperwork (form 4/5) if applicable – consult mortuary or medical examiner.  
  - Refer to Coroner/Procurator Fiscal if:  
    - Unknown cause  
    - Sudden, unexpected  
    - Related to surgery/anaesthesia  
    - Patient not seen by doctor in last 14 days  
    - Occupational disease, violence, neglect, or suicide  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Not applicable – patient is deceased.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- No drugs for death verification.  
- **If reversible cause (e.g., opioid overdose) before declaring death:**  
  - Naloxone 0.4–2 mg IV/IM/IN, repeated every 2 min up to 10 mg if no response – available in ED/ward emergency trolley.  
  - **Do not give naloxone if signs of life are absent for >30 minutes** – it will not reverse anoxic brain injury.  
- **If hypoglycaemia:**  
  - 50 mL 50% dextrose IV. Check blood glucose after 5 minutes.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - Giving naloxone to a patient who is already brain dead will not revive them and wastes critical time. Only give if there is any respiratory effort or pupil reactivity.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Reversible cause missed → premature declaration of death; legal consequences.  
  - Family distress if verification is rushed or poorly communicated.  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Inaccurate death certification → incorrect mortality statistics, potential legal liability.  
  - Failure to identify non‑natural cause → missed referral to coroner.
sections.Prognosis:
- Once death is correctly verified with irreversible brainstem loss, prognosis is zero survival.  
- Reversible causes, if treated within minutes, may have good neurological outcome; but if left untreated progresses to true death.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **DO always check core temperature before pronouncing death in any patient found cold.**  
- **DO perform a full brainstem examination (pupils, corneal, gag, cough, oculocephalic) – not just pulse and breathing.**  
- **DO document clearly the absence of all signs, the time of verification, and the name of the verifying doctor.** Use a standardised proforma if available.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT pronounce death based only on absent pulse and respiration in a patient who could have hypothermia or drug overdose.**  
- **Do NOT write “cardiorespiratory arrest” as cause of death – it is a mechanism, not a cause. Always state the underlying disease (e.g., acute myocardial infarction, pneumonia).**  
- **Do NOT fill Cremation Form 4 without checking the body for pacemaker or radioactive implants – these must be removed first.**
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- (Not applicable – this topic is about death, not discharge of living patient. However, for family:)
- **Return if:** family member develops acute stress reaction, requires bereavement counselling, or has concerns about circumstances of death (e.g., will need to report to coroner).  
- **Follow-up:** GP to arrange bereavement visit within 4–6 weeks.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (Registrar/Consultant) if:**  
  - You are unsure about the cause of death or any details on certification.  
  - Body shows signs of non‑natural injury (suspicious).  
  - Death occurs within 24 hours of admission or during surgery/anaesthesia.  
  - Need to refer to Coroner/Procurator Fiscal.  
- **Call ICU / MET if:** any doubt about reversibility (e.g., patient with signs of life after overdose/hypothermia requires ongoing resuscitation).  
- **Call crash team (2222) if:** during verification you discover any sign of life (e.g., gasp, pulse) – stop the pronouncement and start resuscitation immediately.

rawMarkdown:
## Death Verification & Certification
**One-liner:** Confirm irreversible loss of brainstem function, follow legal documentation, and avoid declaring death in reversible causes (hypothermia, overdose, hypothyroidism).

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Reversible mimic — severe hypothermia (core temp <30 °C) or drug overdose (opioids, benzos, tricyclics):**  
  The single finding that says "this is a killer, not death": **pupils may be fixed/dilated, but deep tendon reflexes or spontaneous respiratory effort (even gasping) can still be present. Do NOT pronounce death until core temp >32 °C and drug effect is ruled out.**
- **Brainstem death mimic — locked-in syndrome or severe Guillain‑Barré:**  
  The single finding: **patient appears unresponsive but can make vertical eye movements. Elicit sustained upward gaze before pronouncing.**

### Presentation
- **Cardinal signs:**  
  - No palpable pulse (carotid/femoral) for ≥5 minutes  
  - No audible heart sounds  
  - Fixed, dilated pupils unresponsive to light  
  - No respiratory effort for ≥5 minutes  
  - No response to verbal or painful stimuli  
  - Absent corneal, gag, and cough reflexes  
  - No spontaneous movement  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Apnoea test (if performed) – no respiratory effort after stopping ventilation for 5 minutes (only in controlled setting)  
  - Pupils mid‑position (4–6 mm) or fully dilated, no light reflex  
  - Absent oculocephalic ("doll's eye") and oculovestibular (caloric) reflexes  
  - No motor response to supraorbital or nail‑bed pressure

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Death vs. reversible coma (most likely mimic):**  
  - Drug overdose (e.g., opioids, sedatives) – pinpoint pupils (opioids) or flaccid tone, history of access to medications  
  - Hypothermia – core temp <30 °C, shivering may be absent  
  - Severe hypoglycaemia – serum glucose <3 mmol/L, rapid correction restores signs  
  - Hypothyroidism (myxoedema coma) – bradycardia, hypothermia, hypoventilation, thyroid history  
  - Brainstem stroke – may spare some reflexes (e.g., preserved blinking to threat)  
- **Splitter:**  
  **The single clinical feature that differentiates death from reversible coma:** **respiratory drive.** In death – no spontaneous breaths for ≥5 minutes. In reversible coma – gasping or irregular breaths (e.g., Biot’s, Cheyne‑Stokes) may be present. Also: **pupils reactive to light (any constriction) essentially rules out death.**

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**  
  - Check core temperature (rectal, bladder, oesophageal) – must be >32 °C before verification  
  - Continuous ECG or cardiac monitor – look for any electrical activity (P waves, QRS complexes)  
  - Capillary blood glucose – <3 mmol/L treat first (cannot verify death until glucose >3.5 mmol/L)  
  - Pupillary light test with bright torch  
  - Corneal reflex  
  - Gag/cough reflex (suction catheter)  
  - Pain response (supraorbital or nail‑bed pressure)  
  - 5‑minute apnoea observation (spontaneous breathing trial if ventilated – but in non‑ICU setting, just observe)  
- **Bloods:** (only if reversible cause suspected)  
  - Thyroid function, cortisol, toxicology screen  
- **Imaging:**  
  - None for verification (but consider CT head if brainstem death suspected in ICU)  
- **Special tests:**  
  - No routine ancillary tests; in ICU context, may use EEG or angiography for brainstem death  
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
  Use **UK Academy of Medical Royal Colleges (AoMRC) criteria** for confirmation of death (2019 update):  
  - Absence of conscious response  
  - Absent pupillary, corneal, gag, cough, and oculocephalic reflexes  
  - Absent motor responses in cranial nerve distribution  
  - Apnoea (no respiratory effort after disconnecting ventilator for >5 minutes with adequate oxygenation)  
- **Never‑miss test:**  
  **Core temperature measurement** – hypothermia can mimic death and cause fixed pupils/bradycardia. A cold patient is not dead until warm.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **Do NOT pronounce death until reversible causes excluded.**  
  - Check for signs of life for at least 5 minutes (pulse, breathing, pupils, reflexes).  
  - If any sign of life present → start CPR, treat reversible cause (e.g., naloxone for opioid, 50 mL 50% dextrose for hypoglycaemia).  
  - If no signs of life → call supervising clinician or consultant to confirm (if unsure).  
  - If legally dead: record exact time of death, prepare death certificate.  
- **Ward (first 24‑72h):**  
  - No active management for the deceased. For the family: offer private time, bereavement support, ensure proper viewing.  
  - Arrange for “last offices” (patient dignity, removal of lines/tubes if no coroner involvement).  
  - **Certify and complete Medical Certificate of Cause of Death (MCCD) within 24 hours if cause is natural and clear.**  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Cremation paperwork (form 4/5) if applicable – consult mortuary or medical examiner.  
  - Refer to Coroner/Procurator Fiscal if:  
    - Unknown cause  
    - Sudden, unexpected  
    - Related to surgery/anaesthesia  
    - Patient not seen by doctor in last 14 days  
    - Occupational disease, violence, neglect, or suicide  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Not applicable – patient is deceased.

### Drugs & Doses
- No drugs for death verification.  
- **If reversible cause (e.g., opioid overdose) before declaring death:**  
  - Naloxone 0.4–2 mg IV/IM/IN, repeated every 2 min up to 10 mg if no response – available in ED/ward emergency trolley.  
  - **Do not give naloxone if signs of life are absent for >30 minutes** – it will not reverse anoxic brain injury.  
- **If hypoglycaemia:**  
  - 50 mL 50% dextrose IV. Check blood glucose after 5 minutes.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - Giving naloxone to a patient who is already brain dead will not revive them and wastes critical time. Only give if there is any respiratory effort or pupil reactivity.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Reversible cause missed → premature declaration of death; legal consequences.  
  - Family distress if verification is rushed or poorly communicated.  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Inaccurate death certification → incorrect mortality statistics, potential legal liability.  
  - Failure to identify non‑natural cause → missed referral to coroner.

### Prognosis
- Once death is correctly verified with irreversible brainstem loss, prognosis is zero survival.  
- Reversible causes, if treated within minutes, may have good neurological outcome; but if left untreated progresses to true death.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **DO always check core temperature before pronouncing death in any patient found cold.**  
- **DO perform a full brainstem examination (pupils, corneal, gag, cough, oculocephalic) – not just pulse and breathing.**  
- **DO document clearly the absence of all signs, the time of verification, and the name of the verifying doctor.** Use a standardised proforma if available.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT pronounce death based only on absent pulse and respiration in a patient who could have hypothermia or drug overdose.**  
- **Do NOT write “cardiorespiratory arrest” as cause of death – it is a mechanism, not a cause. Always state the underlying disease (e.g., acute myocardial infarction, pneumonia).**  
- **Do NOT fill Cremation Form 4 without checking the body for pacemaker or radioactive implants – these must be removed first.**

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- (Not applicable – this topic is about death, not discharge of living patient. However, for family:)
- **Return if:** family member develops acute stress reaction, requires bereavement counselling, or has concerns about circumstances of death (e.g., will need to report to coroner).  
- **Follow-up:** GP to arrange bereavement visit within 4–6 weeks.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (Registrar/Consultant) if:**  
  - You are unsure about the cause of death or any details on certification.  
  - Body shows signs of non‑natural injury (suspicious).  
  - Death occurs within 24 hours of admission or during surgery/anaesthesia.  
  - Need to refer to Coroner/Procurator Fiscal.  
- **Call ICU / MET if:** any doubt about reversibility (e.g., patient with signs of life after overdose/hypothermia requires ongoing resuscitation).  
- **Call crash team (2222) if:** during verification you discover any sign of life (e.g., gasp, pulse) – stop the pronouncement and start resuscitation immediately.
